name: Cleveland-Cliffs Inc.

profile.description: Cleveland-Cliffs Inc. is the largest flat-rolled steel producer in North America and the largest supplier of steel to the automotive industry. The vertically integrated company operates across iron ore mining, pellet production, direct reduced iron, steelmaking, and downstream finishing, stamping, tooling, and tubing. Formed through the transformative acquisitions of AK Steel (2020), ArcelorMittal USA (2020), Ferrous Processing and Trading (2021), and Stelco (2024) under CEO Lourenco Goncalves. Founded in 1847 and headquartered in Cleveland, Ohio.

innovation.label: DEVELOPING
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): CEO Lourenco Goncalves engineered a transformative industry consolidation through four major acquisitions since 2020 -- AK Steel, ArcelorMittal USA, FPT, and Stelco -- converting Cleveland-Cliffs from an iron ore miner into the largest flat-rolled steel producer in North America and the #1 supplier to the auto industry [2]. The world's most modern Direct Reduction plant in Toledo (completed 2020) demonstrates execution on next-gen steelmaking. AI Deployment (0/2): No AI-powered products or quantified AI-driven cost savings identified; the steel industry remains primarily physical-process-driven with limited AI adoption [2]. Market Share (2/2): Dominant #1 position in North American flat-rolled steel and #1 automotive steel supplier, with the vertically integrated model from iron ore mining through finished stamped components creating structural competitive advantages [1][2]. Product Pipeline (1/2): Stelco integration provides capacity expansion and cost synergies; tariff protection on steel imports is creating favorable domestic pricing dynamics with imports at lowest levels since the GFC [1]. However, the cancellation of the $1.6B EAF/DRI Middletown project signals limited green steel pipeline [3]. R&D Intensity (0/1): R&D spending is at industry standard; the company has not differentiated itself as a technology innovator within steelmaking.

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (2/4): 'We are steelmakers' represents a clear identity that connects the company's work to a vital national industry. Goncalves frames Cleveland-Cliffs' purpose around American manufacturing sovereignty and preserving domestic steelmaking capability, which is specific and genuine but does not articulate a transformative vision beyond the industry's current boundaries [2]. Community Impact (1/2): Cleveland-Cliffs is a major employer in Cleveland, Ohio and across rust belt communities, with union relationships (USW) providing employment stability. The company received the Willy Korf/Ken Iverson Steel Vision Award for contributions to the industry [2]. However, no distinctive charitable or community programs were identified beyond standard corporate citizenship. Employee Retention (0/2): No specific retention or tenure data publicly available; the company's 25,000 employees are primarily unionized steelworkers with retention driven by union contracts rather than mission alignment [2]. Insider Buying (1/2): Goncalves is known to maintain significant personal stock holdings in CLF, demonstrating financial alignment with shareholders; he received the 'CEO/Chairperson of the Year' Global Metals Award in 2021 for peer/investor trust [2].

infrastructure.label: DEVELOPING
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on available metrics reflecting cyclical trough conditions. Revenue of $18.61B in 2025 declined from $22.99B in 2022, yielding a negative 5-year revenue CAGR of approximately -6.8% [1][4]. ROE is deeply negative at -18.6% due to net losses driven by weak steel pricing and high energy costs, and profit margin is -6.4% [1]. Debt-to-equity of 129.3% reflects $7.8B total debt against only $45M cash, creating significant financial leverage risk [4]. Q1 2026 steelmaking revenues of $4.76B were up from $4.47B in Q1 2025, showing modest sequential improvement, but losses continued with management expecting sequential quarterly improvement through 2026 [1]. The bright spot is a Forward PEG of 0.43 as analysts expect an earnings recovery, and steel tariffs are providing pricing support with imports at their lowest levels since the GFC [1]. Forward P/E of 31.0x is elevated but reflects trough earnings rather than overvaluation.

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (8/9): Cleveland-Cliffs' core business -- iron ore mining, blast furnace steelmaking, and downstream metal processing -- is structurally immune to AI disruption [1][2]. Steel production requires massive physical infrastructure (blast furnaces, rolling mills, stamping operations), skilled labor, raw material inputs (iron ore, coal, scrap), and regulatory compliance that AI cannot substitute. The vertically integrated model from mining through finished automotive components creates layers of physical-world operations beyond AI's reach. AI may even increase demand for steel through data center construction, electrical grid expansion, and infrastructure buildout. The only risk is AI-optimized competitors improving yield and efficiency, but this is marginal rather than existential. AI Opportunity (0/1): No meaningful AI-derived revenue. Cleveland-Cliffs does not sell AI-related products or services, and no quantified AI-driven operational savings have been reported.

infinite_game.summary: Cleveland-Cliffs under Goncalves exhibits a paradoxical mix of infinite and finite game characteristics. The extraordinary willingness to transform the company through bold acquisitions (iron ore to integrated steel) demonstrates genuine existential flexibility, and the courage to take on massive debt during COVID to consolidate the industry is undeniable. However, Goncalves' confrontational leadership style, aggressive treatment of competitors and analysts on earnings calls, and focus on industry dominance over organizational purpose make this more of a 'winning' orientation than a 'keep playing' one.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (1/3): Goncalves articulates a vision of American steelmaking sovereignty -- protecting domestic manufacturing capability from foreign competition -- which resonates as a purpose beyond pure profit [2]. However, this reads more as a competitive positioning ('we must beat foreign steel') than a Sinek-style Just Cause about a better future. The company's language is fundamentally about winning and dominating, not about serving a higher purpose. 'We are steelmakers' is an identity statement, not a Just Cause.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (0/2): Goncalves is known for a confrontational, aggressive leadership style that is the antithesis of Sinek's Circle of Safety. His earnings calls are frequently combative with analysts, and his management approach is described as top-down and demanding [2]. While the USW union relationship provides some employee protection, the culture appears driven by fear of the CEO rather than psychological safety. Rapid acquisition-driven growth (4 major deals in 4 years) creates organizational instability as cultures are forcibly merged.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (0/1): Goncalves exhibits a classic finite competitive mindset -- his public statements consistently frame competitors (Nucor, foreign steel producers) as adversaries to defeat rather than rivals to learn from [2]. His aggressive rhetoric about imports, competitors, and industry critics reflects a 'winning' orientation rather than Sinek's concept of using rivals' strengths to improve one's own weaknesses.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (2/2): Cleveland-Cliffs under Goncalves executed one of the most dramatic industry transformations in recent U.S. industrial history -- converting from a pure iron ore mining company into the largest vertically integrated flat-rolled steel producer in North America through four acquisitions totaling billions in enterprise value [2]. The pivot from mining to manufacturing required fundamental organizational reinvention, new capabilities, and willingness to abandon the company's historical identity. This is directly analogous to Sinek's canonical examples of existential flexibility.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (2/2): Goncalves demonstrated extraordinary courage by acquiring AK Steel and ArcelorMittal USA during the COVID-19 pandemic when steel prices were depressed and the industry outlook was bleak [2]. These multi-billion-dollar bets required taking on massive debt during the most uncertain economic period in decades. The subsequent acquisition of FPT (2021) and Stelco (2024) continued the pattern of bold, long-term capability building. The Toledo DRI plant ($1B+ investment) was a decade-long bet on next-gen steelmaking technology. Multiple examples of leadership making costly long-term bets.

investment_thesis: Cleveland-Cliffs is the dominant North American flat-rolled steel producer trading at cyclical trough valuations, with a PEG ratio of 0.43 reflecting consensus expectations for a significant earnings recovery as steel tariffs provide pricing support and imports hit their lowest levels since the global financial crisis [1]. Q1 2026 steelmaking revenues of $4.76B were up 6.5% YoY, and management expects each quarter to improve sequentially as order book momentum translates to earnings [1]. The vertically integrated model from iron ore mining through finished automotive stamped components provides cost advantages and customer stickiness, with CLF as the #1 steel supplier to the U.S. auto industry [2]. At $13.60/share (beta 2.0), the stock offers significant leverage to any steel price recovery.
thesis_continued: The bear case is a levered balance sheet meeting a cyclical trough. Debt-to-equity of 129.3% with $7.8B total debt against only $45M cash creates significant financial risk if steel prices remain depressed or decline further [4]. Operating cash flow was negative $436M in 2025, free cash flow was negative $60.5M, and the company posted trailing EPS of -$2.31 with ROE of -18.6% [4]. The cancelled $1.6B green steel project in Middletown (which had a $500M DOE grant) signals limited commitment to decarbonization and removes a key long-term growth catalyst [3]. The company emits approximately 4.2M metric tons of GHG annually from the Cleveland Works facility alone, creating ESG headwinds and potential future carbon cost liabilities [3]. Revenue has declined from $22.99B in 2022 to $18.61B in 2025, and the 5-year revenue CAGR of -6.8% reflects structural market contraction rather than purely cyclical dynamics.
key_catalysts: Steel tariff enforcement with imports at lowest levels since the GFC, combined with derivative product enforcement, provides structural pricing support for domestic producers and could drive margin recovery in H2 2026

Stelco integration synergies and sequential quarterly improvement in order book and pricing, with management guiding for improving profitability through FY2026 as price realization catches up to spot market
GM Supplier of the Year award and deepening automotive relationships position CLF to benefit from onshoring trends and growing EV production requirements for high-grade flat-rolled steel
key_risks: Highly leveraged balance sheet with $7.8B debt, only $45M cash, and negative operating cash flow creates financial fragility -- any prolonged steel price downturn could force asset sales or dilutive equity raises

Cancelled $1.6B Middletown green steel project and 4.2M metric tons annual GHG emissions from Cleveland Works create growing ESG liability as carbon regulations tighten and institutional investors divest carbon-intensive holdings

Cyclical steel industry with 5-year revenue CAGR of -6.8%, negative profitability, and beta of 2.0 means the stock could significantly underperform in a recession or if tariff protections are reversed
